text: The surgeon makes an incision around the deep ring of an inguinal scrotal hernia, dissociating the distal sac from the peritoneum. Dense adhesions are visible on the medial aspect of the deep ring. A standard flap development is performed, maintaining separation between the parietal and visceral compartments, medial and lateral respectively.
The surgeon instructs residents to focus on dissecting within the visceral compartment towards the deep ring, where they will soon encounter the intentionally created peritoneal hole.

The surgeon reflects anatomical structures laterally towards the side wall to establish a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, indicating the point reached after reducing the hernia sac without further dissection into the canal.

The surgeon transitions to developing the myopectenial orifice (MPO), reducing femoral lymphatic tissue. The vasovasorum of the vas deferens, obliterated umbilical artery, and medial ligament are visible as the surgeon works to create a solid landing zone for mesh placement.

The surgeon works around the obturator foramen, parietalizing the vasculature and developing a lateral pocket. The iliopsoas muscle is identified, and the chord elements, including the vas deferens, are parietalized. The prominent prostate is visible, and the surgeon evaluates and identifies possible cord lipomas.

The surgeon examines the transversalis sling, identifying cord elements such as the vas deferens and associated vasculature. Fat tissue is observed near the area, with differentiation between cord lipomas and iliac lymphatic fat, which penetrates posterior to the sling. The surgeon notes that the iliac lymphatic fat should be left undisturbed to avoid lymphoceles or nerve injury.

The surgeon identifies a small ribbon of fat overlying the posterior transversalis sling and carefully pulls on it to explore for a potential missed lipoma. The exploration reveals a distal lipoma connected to other fat tissue.

The surgeon manipulates areolar tissues adhered to the distal sac, retracting them back to reveal a clear view of the anatomical structures. An extra-large mid-weight mesh is positioned and secured laterally near the most prominent defect, followed by a standard flap closure.
The surgeon closes the flap from lateral to medial, addressing the visible fenestration. The closure appears straightforward, similar to other fenestrations encountered in preperitoneal dissections.
